coredata.dc:title: From blue to near infrared: Carbon dots full-color tunable lasers for speckle free laser imaging

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenfei
Chen, Jingsong
Ni, Yiqun
Zhang, Shaofeng
Li, Ling
Yan, Peiguang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of novel luminescent material for multi-color lasers duo to abundant of emission wavelength. The CDs tunable lasers had ether narrow tunable wavelength or complicated fabrication in previous publications. In this study, a simple one-pot solvothermal method is employed to synthesize four types of fluorescent CDs, emitting blue, green, orange-red, and near-infrared light, with photoluminescence covering a range from 445 nm to 721 nm by CDs surface modulation. Random lasers emitted from blue to near infrared regime are achieved by using CDs as gain medium. The CDs lasers are also employed as laser imaging light sources, enabling speckle-free full-color (from blue to near infrared) laser imaging. Images quality are found to be significant higher than that using traditional lasers or LEDs as light sources under the same circumstances. This work provides a simple one-pot fabrication method to develop wide band tunable CDs lasers and promotes the application of CDs lasers in full-color speckle-free laser imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
One-pot method
Photoluminescence
Lasing
Speckle free laser imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation schemes for four types of CDs; (b)-(e) Random lasing spectra of 400/500/600/700-CDs; (f)-(i) Image of speckle-free full-color laser imaging based on 400/500/600/700-CD laser.

Morphology of CDs. (a),(c),(e) and (g) TEM images (inset showing the High-resolution TEM images), (b),(d),(f) and (h) enlarge TEM images (insets show particle size distribution statistical data based on 200 particles) of 400/500/600/700-CDs.
Structure and optical property of CDs. (a) Raman spectra; (b) XRD patterns; (c) Absorption a; (d) FT-IR spectra of 400/500/600/700-CDs.

Composition and photoluminescence of CDs. (a) XPS spectra; (b)-(e) High-resolution C 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra; (f) Fluorescence emission spectrum (inset showing the normalized emission spectrum) of 400/500/600/700-CDs.

Lasing characteristics of CDs lasers. (a),(d),(g),(j) Random lasing spectra of microcavity under different power (inset showing the photographs of their laser spot); (b),(e),(h),(k) input excitation power versus output intensity of random lasing (inset showing the photographs of microcavity); (c),(f),(i),(l) random laser threshold versus the area of the excitation laser spot of 400/500/600/700-CDs.

Speckle-free full-color laser imaging based on CD lasers. Optical images of 1951 USAF resolution test charts showing laser emission from (a) 400-CDs, (b) 500-CDs, (c) 600-CDs, (d) 700-CDs; (e)-(h)single wavelength laser sources and (i)-(l)light emitting diode sources. (m) Experimental setup of speckle free laser imaging. (n) Schematic configuration of the laser cavity.
XPS data analyses of the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of four CDs.
Comparison of performance of imaging by using different types of light sources.

head.ce:title: From blue to near infrared: Carbon dots full-color tunable lasers for speckle free laser imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of novel luminescent material for multi-color lasers duo to abundant of emission wavelength. The CDs tunable lasers had ether narrow tunable wavelength or complicated fabrication in previous publications. In this study, a simple one-pot solvothermal method is employed to synthesize four types of fluorescent CDs, emitting blue, green, orange-red, and near-infrared light, with photoluminescence covering a range from 445 nm to 721 nm by CDs surface modulation. Random lasers emitted from blue to near infrared regime are achieved by using CDs as gain medium. The CDs lasers are also employed as laser imaging light sources, enabling speckle-free full-color (from blue to near infrared) laser imaging. Images quality are found to be significant higher than that using traditional lasers or LEDs as light sources under the same circumstances. This work provides a simple one-pot fabrication method to develop wide band tunable CDs lasers and promotes the application of CDs lasers in full-color speckle-free laser imaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A simple one-pot solvothermal method is developed to synthesize four types of fluorescent CDs, emitting blue, green, orange-red, and near-infrared light. Full-color tunable CDs lasers with emission wavelength from blue to near infrared are realized. High quality speckle free laser imaging is achieved at multiple wavelength by using the tunable CDs lasers as light sources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have found wide applications in 3D-printing[1], biomedicine [2], energy storage[3]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of novel nano-material and have captured intensive research interest due to their unique optical properties[4] and simple fabrication[5]. CDs have also found extensive applications in lighting [6,7], bioinaging[8] and anti-counterfeiting[9], etc. With the advancement of optical display, there is an increasing demand for new imaging light sources [10]. Compared to traditional light sources, CDs random lasers offer selectable emission wavelengths with highe photon density, while avoiding speckle interference. The high brightness, wavelength tunability, and free of laser speckles make them be a candidate for high quality laser imaging light sources.

CDs lasers have been extensively investigate by different researchers in the past few years. The first CDs laser and first tunable CDs lasers were reported by our research group in 2012 [11]and 2014 [12]. Qu et al. reported Fabry-Perot laser emission of green light [13]. Yuan et al. achieved CDs lasersemitted in three different colors, expanding the tuning range of CDs lasers to the entire visible light [14]. CDs lasers with spectral coverage from 450 nm to 630 nm were also realized in 2022[15]. The broadest wavelength tunability of CDs lasers range from 315 to 600 nm was also reported by our group in 2022. [16] Emission wavelength of CDs lasers extended to 643.9 nm was reported by Lu et al.[17] The wide band CDs lasers emitting from blue to near-infrared region were first reported by Lu etc. in 2023.[18] However, research on wide-band CDs lasers, especially in the near-infrared region, remains relatively scarce. Fabrication of wide band tunable CDs lasers are also complicated. Wide band emission CDs fabricated by simple one-pot method are significant in understanding evolution of structure of CDs with emission wavelength.

Four types of CDs were prepared (emitting blue, green, orange-red, and near-infrared light) in this work by using a one-pot solvothermal method, with emission peaks covering a range from 445 nm to 721 nm. These four types of CDs were dispersed in epoxy resin with silica scatterers to form CDs-epoxy resin films, which provide optical feedback to achieve tunable random lasing. High-quality full-color speckle-free laser imaging was realized by using the above-mentioned CDs lasers as light sources. This work fills in gap of simple one-pot fabrication of CDs lasers emitted from blue to near-infrared region and provides a demonstration of speckle-free laser imaging.

After the solvothermal process, four types of CDs, whose fluorescence emission peak wavelength located around 452 nm (400-CDs), 560 nm (500-CDs), 618 nm (600-CDs) and 721 nm (700-CDs) were obtained by silica gel column chromatography. The CDs were dispersed in dichloromethane and exhibit pale yellow, yellow, red, and brown color under sunlight as shown in Fig. 1.

Morphology of these four types of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the results are shown in Fig. 2. It is observed that all four types of CDs are quasi-spherical with uniform distribution. Diameters of 400/500/600/700-CDs are found to be 2～7 nm, 2～7 nm, 2～10 nm and 2～10 nm with average diameter of 4.5 nm, 4.7 nm, 5.7 nm and 6.7 nm as shown in the upper right corner of Fig. 2b-h. High-resolution TEM images of the four types of CDs are shown in the upper right corner of Fig. 2a-g. The lattice spacing of the four CDs is measured to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) facet of graphite [19].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of 400/500/600/700-CDs are shown in Fig. 3a. It is observed that all of the CDs exhibit a peak located around 22°. The broad peaks located around 14° are corresponding to increase of amorphous carbon structure (i. e. functional groups) on CDs. This reveals that all the four types of CDs presence a certain degree of graphitization and functional groups are presented on their surface. [20] Sharper peaks in the XRD patterns at 22° indicate higher degree of graphitization. Higher degree of graphitization leads to creation of narrower band gaps between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO).[21,22] This is one of the reasons which leads to red shift of fluorescence emission. Raman spectra of 400/500/600/700-CDs (Fig. 3b) exhibit two dominant bands located at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which are corresponding to surface-related disordered sp3 structure (D band) and graphite sp2 structure (G band), respectively[23,24]. This suggests that graphite structure and surface-related state (i. e. functional groups) coexist in all the four types of CDs, which is coincided with XRD characterization. The peak intensity ratio, Ig/Id, of 400/500/600/700-CDs are calculated to be 1.20, 1.14, 1.1, and 1.03, respectively. This indicates that the proportion of surface functional groups on CDs increases with red shift of fluorescence emission. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of the four types of CDs are shown in Fig. 3c. It can be observed that 400/500/600-CDs exhibit strong absorption in the 250～350 nm region, corresponding to π-π* transitions of CC/C-C in the carbon core and n-π* transitions of CO. A small absorption peak located at 500 nm is also observe from 400/500/600-CDs. The 700-CDs exhibit a broader and stronger absorption bands in the 250～550 nm region than that of 400/500/600-CDs. This is because functional groups lead to the formation of different surface states, which capture excitons, with varying sizes in carbon dots. These excitons lead to a longer wavelength of absorption, which is observed as redshift in the absorption spectra. The presence of multiple surface states with different energy levels contribute to a broadening of the absorption spectra. These results indicate that 600/700-CDs have larger surface states compared to that of 400/500-CDs.

Chemical structures and compositions of CDs were studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3d, all the CDs exhibited similar FTIR spectra, indicating similar graphite core structures and surface functional groups on CDs. For all the four types of CDs, stretching vibration absorption bands of CC, CO, C-O, and O-H were observed at 1450 cm−1, 1700 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, and 3450 cm−1 [24]. This indicates the formation of multi-aromatic structures in CDs. It is noteworthy that the absorption bands of C-O (CO) are significantly stronger (weaker) in 600/700-CDs than that in 400/500-CDs. This indicates that the proportion of surface functional groups in 600/700-CDs is higher than that of 400/500-CDs, which is consistent with the results of Raman spectroscopy. XPS analysis of the four types of CDs in Fig. 4a revealed typical peaks at around 285 eV (C 1 s) and 532 eV (O 1 s).[25] This indicates that all four types of CDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen atoms. The carbon-to-oxygen ratios of 400/500/600/700-CDs were calculated to be 12.6/11.9/10.6/8.9, indicating that the surface functional groups of CDs increase with red shift of fluorescence emission, which is consistent with Raman and FTIR analysis. The high-resolution XPS spectra in Fig. 4b-e show that the C 1 s band could be fitted into three main binding energy peaks of CC/C-C, C-O, and CO, while the O 1 s band could be fitted into two main binding energy peaks of CO and O-H/C-O. Table 1 summarizes the proportions of different functional groups in the four CDs. It can be observed that 600/700-CDs have more -OH groups compared to 400/500-CDs, which is consistent with FT-IR and Raman spectra analysis. According to the analysis, 600/700-CDs also possess more CC/C-C due to larger carbon cores. These results show that all four types of CDs are composed of a carbon core and oxygen-containing functional groups.

Fluorescence spectra and normalized fluorescence spectra of the four types of CDs are shown in Fig. 4f. Fluorescence emission peaks of 400/500/600/700-CDs are found to be located around 452 nm, 560 nm, 618 nm and 721 nm. Quantum yield of 400/500/ 600/700-CDs are measured to be 24.4 %, 31.6 %, 53.6 %, and 6.2 %, respectively.

Based on the above characterization and analysis, it can be concluded that the emission red shift mechanisms of these four types of CDs are closely related to their structures. Higher degree of graphitization leads to creation of narrower band gaps. With red shifts of luminescence, the content of oxygen-containing functional groups (mainly hydroxyl groups), which serve as surface defects, on the surface of 400/500/600/700-CDs gradually increases. These defect energy levels cause lowering of LUMO, resulting in red shifts of luminescence and enabling multicolor emission. [26,27] The higher degree of graphitization together with change of surface state leading to red shifting of emission. Hydroxyl groups increase the degree of delocalization, forming rigid structure, which enhances the intensity of fluorescence emission.

Based on their optical properties, CDs are dispersed in epoxy resin (Hasuncast 3010) to prepare CDs-epoxy resin films. Silica powder (FuChen, 0.5 micrometer) is added into the film as scattering medium. It should be noted that the luminescent properties of CDs are not affected by the epoxy resin. No fluorescence emission is observed from the epoxy resin under the corresponding excitation light. The 400/500/600 CDs-epoxy resin films are directly deposited on quartz slides to form a layer of laser gain medium. The 700 CDs-epoxy resin film is sandwiched between an aluminum film mirror and quartz mirror to form laser cavities. The cavities are pumped by 410/490/560/650 nm laser for 400/500/600/700 CDs. Schematic configuration of the laser cavity is shown in Fig. 6n. Under pumping of pump lasers, electrons transition from lower energy levels to higher energy levels. When population of electrons in high energy levels accumulates to a certain extent, population inversion occurs. Laser is generated if a large number of electrons in the excited state return to ground state. It is observed that only luminescence band is occurred in the emission spectrum when pump power is low. Sharp peaks (i. e.: laser modes) begin to appear in the emission spectra when pump power exceeded a threshold power (12/15/17/27 kW/cm2 for 400/500/600/700 CDs). Meanwhile, laser emission intensity rapidly increases with increasing pump power. As shown in Fig. 5, a knotting inflection point appears in the input-output curve, indicating that stimulated emission begins to dominate in the emission spectrum. It can be observed from Fig. 5a, d, g and j that laser modes are randomly distributed in the emission spectra. This is result from scattering of light by silica powder in the CDs-epoxy resin film. Random distribution of laser modes indicates that random close loop paths are formed in the laser cavities. It also can be observed that more random laser modes emerge with increasing pump power. This is result from more random close loop paths are supported by higher optical gain under higher pump power. It is shown in the excitation area-threshold curves in Fig. 5c, f, I, and l that laser threshold gradually decreases with the increase of excitation area. The diffusion model reveals that in random lasers, the laser threshold Pth varies with the light transport length lt and follows a relationship of Pth∼lt 1/2. Meanwhile, It is related to the gain length lg by It∼1/lg, and Ig is related to the pumping area A by Ig∼A2/3. The relationship between A and Pth can be expressed as 1/Pth∼A2/3. If this relationship holds true, it can be inferred from the side that the collected laser is a random laser [16]. Excitation area plays an important role in path length traveled through amplifying medium, which contributes to total amount of gain in random lasers [28,29]. Increasing excitation area will result in an increase of total gain of random lasers, thereby leading to a decrease of laser threshold. The proportional relationship of 1/Pth and A2/3 as shown in Fig. 5c, f, I, and l indicate that our proposed laser are random lasers.

Stability of the random lasers are investigated by using a certain excitation power (twice the threshold power) to continuous pump the cavity for 120 min. The results (figure S1) shows that emission intensity of all the four laser cavities maintain nearly 100 % of their original intensity. This reveals high stability of the random lasers. Laser threshold in this work are neither very high nor very low compared to other works as shown the comparison in table S1.

Random laser has unique properties of simple construction and low spatial coherence, which are indispensable for laser imaging. Low spatial coherence of light enables high quality images without interference of laser speckles, which are formed by coherent interference, in laser imaging. Based on high photon density with low spatial coherence of CDs random lasers, they are utilized to achieve high quality speckle-free full-color laser imaging. Schematic of speckle free laser imaging experimental setup is shown in Fig. 6m. Random lasing generated from CDs micro-cavity is used as low spatial coherence light source to illuminate the USAF1951 resolution test chart. Fig. 6a-d show images of the USAF1951 resolution test chart by using 400/500/600/700-CDs lasers as illumination light sources. It is seen clearly that all the images show uniform and bright light distribution with high optical resolution regardless of illumination wavelength. No laser speckles are observed from all the images. This is benefited from high photon density with low spatial coherence of random laser.

Traditional coherent laser emitted at 460, 560, 612, 710 nm (Fig. 6e-h) and light emitting diode emitted at 450, 532, 620, 700 nm (Fig. 6i-l) are also used as the light sources for imaging by the same setup for comparison. It can be seen that distinct laser speckles, which significantly reduce imaging quality, emerge from all the four images when coherent lasers are used as light sources. This is result from interferences of high coherent laser. The laser imaging quality can be evaluated by calculating the speckle contrast (C)：C = σI / I* where σI represents the standard deviation of intensity and I* represents the average intensity. Speckle contrast of that illuminated by random laser is calculated to be as low as that of LEDs (< 0.01) compared with that of coherent laser (< 0.35) as shown in Table 2. This indicates that light distribution in images illuminated by CDs random lasers is more uniform than that illuminated by coherent laser on one hand. On the other hand, much lower illumination power is needed for random laser than that of LEDs due to higher photon density. Image brightness is remarkable low, which can be attributed to lower photon density of LED, when illuminated by LEDs. These results indicate that CDs lasers have unique advantages in the field of full-color laser imaging. One of the limitations, which hinders wide application of carbon dots lasers in speckle free imaging, is expensive excitation source used to generate carbon dots random lasers. It is essential to develop laser diode pump sources to generate random laser for wide application of carbon dots based speckle free imaging in the future.

In conclusion, wide emission band CDs, which emitting from blue to near-infrared light were fabricated by using a simple one-pot solvent-thermal method. It was demonstrated that the CDs were utilized as laser gain medium to realize full-color tunable random lasing from blue to near infrared regime. This work simplified fabrication of wide band tunable CDs lasers as well as promoted understanding of photoluminescence wavelength evolution with structure of CDs. Speckle-free full-color laser imaging, which exhibited higher brightness, clarity, and resolution compared to that imaging by traditional coherent lasers and LEDs, was achieved by using CDs lasers as imaging light sources. This work paves the way of developing CDs in optical imaging.

Peiguang Yan: Resources. Ling Li: Resources. Shaofeng Zhang: Resources. Yiqun Ni: Resources. Jingsong Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenfei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and Technology Projects of Shenzhen (20231121100725001), Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics (ZDSYS20210623092006020), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61875127).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.175261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with blue-to-near-infrared lasing for colorful speckle-free laser imaging and dynamical holographic display
